methodology: Rankings reflect group-stage and knockout results through 4 July 2026, weighted by results quality, goal difference, squad availability and path difficulty. Title win chances are illustrative estimates aligned with SoccerStatsHub modelling.

rankings.summary: France are the first elite favourite into the quarter-finals after Kylian Mbappe's 70th-minute penalty beat Paraguay in Philadelphia. The scoreline was tighter than expected and the match was played in extreme heat, but Mbappe now has seven goals at this tournament and France have a confirmed route through Morocco in Foxborough.

Morocco are into a second straight World Cup quarter-final after beating co-hosts Canada 3-0 in Houston. Azzedine Ounahi scored twice and Soufiane Rahimi added the third after replacing injured Ismael Saibari, showing the depth and counter-attacking bite that make the France quarter-final more dangerous than the market may suggest.

Spain remain one of the cleanest two-way teams in the field after beating Austria 3-0 and keeping a fourth straight clean sheet. The ranking holds them just below the confirmed quarter-finalists because Portugal still stand in the way, but their defensive base gives them a strong case to jump back to the top tier if they win the Iberian derby.

Argentina survived Cape Verde rather than controlled them, needing extra time and a Diney Borges own goal to escape one of the great World Cup shocks. Lionel Messi scored and the champions still have the tournament craft to grind through Egypt, but the defensive vulnerability in Miami drops them behind France, Morocco and Spain for now.

Portugal stay high after Cristiano Ronaldo and Goncalo Ramos turned the Croatia tie around, but Spain are the toughest possible last-16 opponent for a side that has still needed late moments to solve knockout pressure. If Portugal beat Spain in Dallas, their attacking depth immediately becomes one of the strongest title cases left.

Brazil remain a major contender but have more questions than France or Spain after needing extra time against Japan and likely losing Lucas Paqueta for the Norway tie. Vinicius Junior and Matheus Cunha give them decisive quality, while Carlo Ancelotti's knockout management keeps them above Norway before the East Rutherford meeting.

Mexico's clean-sheet run and Azteca advantage make them one of the most awkward remaining opponents. They have won every match at home in this tournament without conceding, and England's right-back uncertainty gives Julian Quinones a clear matchup to attack.

England have Harry Kane in form and Quansah available again, but the DR Congo comeback and Reece James doubt leave them outside the top group. The Mexico tie is a major style test: survive the opening altitude surge and England's midfield quality can still take control.

Belgium's comeback against Senegal had the drama of a deep-run team, but the first 85 minutes showed why they remain volatile. The USA tie in Seattle now looks finely balanced, especially with the hosts missing Folarin Balogun but carrying home support.

Norway are no longer just a Haaland story, but his five tournament goals give them the most direct upset route against Brazil. Their unbeaten historical record against the Selecao is a useful subplot, while Julian Ryerson's possible return would help against Brazil's wide pace.

The United States are still alive and have a Seattle home tie, but Balogun's suspension removes their best tournament finisher before Belgium arrive. Malik Tillman's dead-ball quality and the crowd keep the upset case alive.

Switzerland's 2-0 win over Algeria was controlled, mature and exactly the kind of result that gives them a credible path into the quarter-finals. Colombia are a harder opponent than Algeria, but Switzerland's defensive structure has been one of the most reliable in the second tier.

Colombia moved past Ghana 1-0 through Jhon Arias and now meet Switzerland in Vancouver. The attack still leans heavily on Luis Diaz and Arias moments, but the clean sheet and improved control lift them above the remaining low-probability chasers.

Egypt are into the last 16 after holding their nerve against Australia, with Mohamed Salah converting his penalty and Abdelmaguid winning the shoot-out. Argentina are heavy favourites, but Egypt's compact shape and penalty confidence make them more dangerous than their open-play profile suggests.

Paraguay leave after making France uncomfortable in a physical 1-0 defeat under extreme Philadelphia heat. The Germany upset remains one of the tournament's defining shocks, but the lack of attacking volume eventually caught up with them against Mbappe.

Canada's co-host run ended with a 3-0 defeat to Morocco, despite early chances for Jonathan David and Tani Oluwaseyi. The scoreline was harsh on the first-half performance, but Morocco punished the spaces Canada left once they chased the game.

Australia were knocked out by Egypt on penalties after a 1-1 draw in Dallas. Mat Ryan was introduced for the shoot-out but could not save any of Egypt's four kicks, while Harry Souttar and Lucas Herrington missed for the Socceroos.

Cape Verde produced one of the tournament's great performances in defeat, twice equalising against Argentina before a 111th-minute own goal ended their run. Sidny Lopes Cabral's extra-time equaliser and Vozinha's eight saves will keep them near the top of the tournament story list.

Ghana's tournament ended in a 1-0 defeat to Colombia, with Jhon Arias scoring early and Ghana unable to create enough pressure to recover. Their draw with England remains the high point, but the knockout attack never found the same threat.

DR Congo pushed England to the brink in Atlanta before Harry Kane's late double sealed a 2-1 win for the Three Lions. Brian Cipenga's early strike and Lionel Mpasi's heroics in goal showed Sébastien Desabre's side belonged at this stage, but their first knockout campaign ended one comeback short.

Senegal led Belgium 2-0 with five minutes remaining through Habib Diarra and Ismaila Sarr before Romelu Lukaku and Youri Tielemans forced extra time, and a controversial VAR-awarded penalty in the 125th minute ended their tournament in Seattle. Aliou Cisse's side played without Edouard Mendy and will rue one of the most painful collapses in World Cup knockout history.

Luka Modric's fifth World Cup ended in Toronto when Portugal came from behind to win 2-1, with Josko Gvardiol's stoppage-time equaliser disallowed by VAR after a connected-ball review. Ivan Perisic had given Croatia the lead in a match featuring two 40-year-old outfielders, but Zlatko Dalic's ageing squad could not hold on.

Ralf Rangnick's Austria reached their first World Cup knockout stage since 1954 but were comprehensively beaten 3-0 by Spain at SoFi Stadium on 2 July. Alexander Schlager made six saves to keep the score respectable, but Austria managed just one real chance as substitute Sasa Kalajdzic headed over.

Algeria held Argentina to a draw in the group stage but were beaten 2-0 by Switzerland in Vancouver on 2 July, ending their wait for a first World Cup knockout victory. Vahid Halilhodzic's side had more possession but managed just 0.73 expected goals and rarely threatened after Dan Ndoye doubled the Swiss lead seconds after half-time.

Bosnia reached the knockouts for the first time after beating Qatar 3-1, with Edin Dzeko still the focal point at 40. A Round of 32 tie with co-hosts USA at Levi's Stadium ended in a 2-0 defeat after Folarin Balogun and Malik Tillman scored, closing a historic campaign that included a draw with Canada.

Sebastian Beccacece's side stunned Germany 2-1 in the group stage but could not repeat the trick at the Estadio Azteca, losing 2-0 to Mexico on 1 July. Piero Hincapie's stoppage-time red card compounded a miserable evening for a team that had already shown they can trouble elite opposition.

Sweden advanced as a best third-placed side but were comprehensively beaten 3-0 by France at MetLife Stadium on 30 June, conceding seven goals across their four tournament matches. Graham Potter admitted it was no disgrace to lose to this France team, but the defensive frailties exposed against the Netherlands returned in New Jersey.

The Elephants reached their first World Cup knockout stage and fought back through Amad Diallo's equaliser in Dallas, only for Erling Haaland to break their hearts with an 86th-minute winner. Franck Kessie and Sebastien Haller provided experience, but Norway's ruthlessness ended Ivory Coast's best run in a generation.
